title: Scientism, Romanticism, and Psychotherapy
subtitle: Three Avenues Through Which Buddhism Enters Western Society
paragraphs: Now I'm going to talk about things that I know virtually nothing about. That's a good start. Really, I'm just trying to see what some people call the gates—I prefer the term avenues—down which Buddhism enters into our society. We can't help but filter out what we don't want and see the Buddha Dhamma from a particular point, because that's the way we're conditioned.

When Buddhism went to China, for instance, it became seen through Taoism, and it took quite a long time for people to realise that Buddhism wasn't quite the same as Taoism, although there were obviously connections. It's the same with us. There are obvious connections with our own thought, but in a sense they can obscure the fundamental teachings of the Buddha.
There are three areas where I'm going to try and point this out. The first is science. The second is romanticism. And the third is psychotherapy.

We have to make a distinction between science, which is the exploration of our physical world, and scientism, which is the philosophy drawn from that. These days, scientific materialism is really what takes over most people who are involved in science. Rupert Sheldrake, whom some of you might know, has just written a book about spiritual practices, and he was saying that it was rare to get a group of scientists who weren't materialists.

One of the leading figures is the biologist E.O. Wilson. He's famed for starting a whole new discipline on the social behaviour of animals and humans—the biological aspects of social behaviour. He wrote a book called Consilience, which means the unity of knowledge. It's where all knowledge comes together and forms some sort of whole concept.

This is what he says: from the birth of stars to the workings of social institutions, all are based on material processes that are ultimately reducible, however long and tortuous the sequence, to the laws of physics.

If you were to create a hierarchy, looking upwards, the first thing you see is physics. It's about stones, the physics of life. From that you get chemistry, from that you get biology, then psychology, sociology, and so on. Everything looks as though it's all arisen from this physical base—and that's what he's basically saying. Interestingly enough, he remains an agnostic as to whether God exists or not.

Here we have a couple of problems. The first is that there's no way of finding out, no way they can tell, how the objective physical world of chemicals and neurons and all those things that we can test translates into the subjective world that we actually experience—emotions, images, moods, sensations. There's a special word for those things: they're called qualia, or as we might understand it, phenomenal experiences. And there's no understanding as to how the flow of consciousness arises out of that base.

It's a presumption—a presumption that everything we experience internally is arising out of matter. That's not, of course, the tradition of all the spiritual traditions. They tell us that the mind is something separate, something other. In Buddhism too it's something other, but of course still falls under the three characteristics—well, the main two: impermanent and insubstantial, not-self.

If you want to read it, there's a great essay by the philosopher Professor Nagel, in which he tries to point this out by asking: what does it feel like to be a bat? A bat sees things through hearing. It can actually tell what insect it's going for and whether to eat it or not through sound—the sensitivity is quite remarkable. What he's saying is that it's impossible for him ever to know what it feels like to be a bat, because the inner experience is so different from the way we experience things.

All this has brought about a rethink about the whole problem. On one side, you'll get philosophers and scientists who talk about reductionism—everything will eventually be proven to arise out of subatomic particles. But there are people who are beginning to question that.

There's a very good talk on TED by a philosopher called David Chalmers. He's positing that consciousness is actually part and parcel of the universe, just as matter, time, and space are—and consciousness alongside them. He's very careful not to move into a sort of pantheism—God is everywhere, anything like that. He doesn't want to overstep the line. But in this way, he's saying that consciousness, being embedded in the whole process of the universe, begins at the very base as a sort of proto-consciousness, and it grows.

This reminds me of another philosopher—a Jesuit, a Christian philosopher who was also a palaeontologist: Teilhard de Chardin. Because of his study of skulls and ancient skeletons, his word for this growing consciousness was the encephalisation of consciousness. As consciousness grew, the brain had to grow bigger. And really, we're now looking down upon what we experience, not from below upwards, from stones upwards, but looking down from consciousness. That gives us a slightly different view of things.

From the Buddha's perspective, we are definitely made up of three parts. First, our physical nature, which can only be known through the mind at the contact base. When we chant the Dependent Origination in the morning, it's that point of contact where we are, shall we say, touching on the physical nature of the universe. And when you think about it, it's quite remarkable—the eyes are actually sensitive to subatomic particles. It's just absolutely amazing. And the tongue is sensitive to chemicals and things like that. So there is this point of contact.

But then from that, it's taken into what's known as the mind door, and it's the mind door that figures it all out, that puts it into some sort of definition—this is curry, or I can hear a car. At the ear door, all you're getting is pressure. The airwaves are just pressure. Everything else is taken back into the mind door. And then from that point of recognising what it is, you give it meaning. A car begins to have meaning—it's a taxi coming for me, and all that. So all that is mind.

And there is this third quality of the Nibbānic element—that's how it's referred to in our school. In later schools, of course, they tended to personalise it and call it Buddha nature, things like that. That was because there was a shift of emphasis. The Buddha himself is very wary of saying it's somebody or something even. He's much more directed to the actual experience and describing the experience of it.

The next thing I want to point out is another book. There's a man called Yuval Harari who's written two books, and they're really fantastic. One is Sapiens and the other is Homo Deus. Sapiens is about how we got to this point, the growth of humans. Homo Deus is where we're going to go from here. One of his big things is to point out that people are actually spending a lot of money so that we don't die. There's a lot of research going on to stop us dying.

One thing I learned from him was this word algorithm, which I'd always thought was something very mystical. I thought, Google has this amazing algorithm and it can find anything you want in milliseconds—so I'd always presumed it really belonged to the area of mysticism. But it turns out that an algorithm is actually just a menu. Take baking bread: when you put that menu into a machine, the machine bakes bread for you. That's its algorithm. So that was an enormous point of enlightenment for me.

Once we've understood that, when biologists look at nature now, they see this process of algorithms. They just see menus being worked out from the very seed to the plant and so on. And so a huge question has come up: what purpose in evolution is consciousness? There was no purpose for it. Because now we have artificial intelligence that can do everything we can do, thinking-wise, mathematically-wise, much faster and much better. And they never tire, never get headaches.

So you've got all these amazing machines that we've created, and they tell us there's some mismatch in our thinking about where consciousness is. Does a machine that does all your accounts have consciousness? This has really created a problem for them.

Of course, this goes back in our history right to the Enlightenment—remember the scientific revolution. If you go back a little bit further, actually, to St. Thomas Aquinas, the Christian theologian, he determined that animals don't have souls. That's when Christianity came to the conclusion that, philosophically speaking, animals don't have souls. Only human beings have souls.

So when we get to Descartes and science, animals don't have sensibility. In the seventeenth century, people were experimenting on live animals without any sense of wrongdoing, because even though the animals were screaming, they really weren't suffering any pain.

There's this magazine, The Week, which summarises the news for us. It always has a little article about science. And I kid you not: one week, a bunch of scientists from somewhere finally discovered—or had proven—that fish do not feel pain. The very next week, another bunch of scientists proved that they did. How you can prove anything which is happening inside us, within our own selves, is a mystery. And all they're looking at is behaviour.

When it comes to these views, on the one side, remember you've also got the eternalist view. I'm quoting here from a commentary, which is sometimes clearer because they're defining things. Therein, what is an eternalist view? That the self and the world are eternal. Now when the Buddha talks about the world, the all, he's really only talking about the five khandhas. He doesn't go beyond that. The great thing I've found about the Buddha is that he doesn't go where there's only inference. That's why there's no supernatural stuff or anything like that—unless you consider things like levitation supernatural. But he doesn't go beyond what you can actually experience.

That's why he never talks about what it is that experiences Nibbāna—because it's a total experience. In the experience itself, there's nobody there who can tell you about it. Afterwards you can reflect upon it, but while you're in a state of transcendent Nibbāna, all he can do is describe what it's like, what it doesn't have.

The commentary goes on to say that this form of view—and this is actually from the scriptures—is a thicket of views, a wilderness of views, a contortion of views, a writhing of views, a fetter of views, a seizure, holding on, inclination, hanging on, false path, wrong way, wrongness, sectarian guide, inverted seizure. This is what is called an eternalist view.

On the one side, you have this idea that something within us—the mind, the heart, emotions, the body and all that, how we experience ourselves phenomenally—that there's something in it which is eternal. And of course the Buddha is pointing out that that's precisely what the great discourse on anattā, not-self, is asking you to see. If what you're experiencing is impermanent, how can you possibly be it?

The other side is what we've just been talking about: the annihilationist view, that the self and the world will be annihilated. This again forms a view, a thicket of views, a wilderness of views, a contortion of views, a writhing of views, a fetter of views, a seizure, holding on, inclination, hanging on, false path, wrong way, wrongness, sectarian guide, inverted seizure. It's called annihilationist view.

To support this, there is of course a very famous quote. Everybody knows it, but they usually only quote the opening lines. It comes from the Udāna, a collection of the Buddha's inspired verses: I have heard that on one occasion—this would be Ānanda speaking—the Blessed One was staying near Sāvatthī at Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika's monastery. On that occasion, the Blessed One was instructing, urging, rousing and encouraging the monks with Dhamma talk concerned with liberation. And the monks, receptive, attentive, focusing their entire awareness, lending an ear, listened to the Dhamma.
By the way, the commentaries point out that when they use the word monks, they mean anybody who's listening—not just monastics.

Then, on realising the significance of that, the Blessed One exclaimed: There is, monks, an unborn, unbecome, unmade, unconditioned. The way the word is used is what we would call a gerund—using an adjective as a noun. So "there is an unborn," not "something unborn." He's very careful not to solidify it into some idea of a person or a self. He says there is an unborn, unbecome, unmade, unconditioned. Everything in the world is conditioned—in other words, dependent on something else for it to arise. We're dependent on air, for instance. But this is completely unconditioned.

Then he says—and this is the bit that people don't usually quote: If there were not the unborn, the unbecome, the unmade, the unconditioned, there would be no way of escape from the born, the become, the made, the conditioned. You could not discern that escape. And he says: Precisely because of this, there is an unborn, unbecome, unmade, and unconditioned. And this is the escape. That's the fullness of the phrase.

It's very difficult to deny, in my mind, that he's talking about something transcendent. But people do. That's all you can say. So that's to do with all this business of transcendence, annihilation, and the reductionism of scientism.

The Buddha does present us with a further problem, though. In his time there were annihilationists. There was a man called Ajita Kesakambalī, a Lokāyata materialist. His line was the usual thing: on death the body's made of earth, you just return to the earth when you die, so you may as well enjoy life while you can.

In the opening of the first discourse, the Buddha says that sensual pleasure—the seeking of sensual pleasure—is one of those extremes we shouldn't be following. But if you're a real materialist, if you really, really think that you've only got this life, then you should be heading for as much enjoyment as you can. Get as much sex, drugs and rock and roll as you can. Why not?

The other side of scientism, of course, is the ethics. Here's Einstein, believe it or not: A man's actions are determined by necessity—it's all conditioned. External and internal. So that in God's eyes, he cannot be responsible any more than an inanimate object is responsible for the motions it goes through.

This actually had a great effect in America during the 1930s, where people got off on these grounds. I was trying to find the particular case I'm thinking of, but couldn't—a murderer was let off on the grounds that he had no choice. The conditions he was in, the way he was brought up, and so on. He walked free and then committed murder again. So there's Einstein for you. It's funny he should talk about God's eyes.

Nietzsche says it in his usual rather roundabout way, but he gets the point across too. He says that if a man finally succeeds in obtaining philosophical conviction of the unconditional necessity of all actions—in other words, conditioned actions—and of their complete irresponsibility, and gets this into his system, any remnants of pricks of conscience disappear. Why would you have shame? Why would you have guilt? You were forced to pull the man's ears off. It just happened.

In the Buddha's time you got this similar sort of thing. Makkhali Gosāla was a fatalist—powerless, life was predestined, completely conditioned. That was his teaching. And of course that isn't the Buddha's.

The Buddha's teaching is not free will—it's not free will—but our growing discernment tells us that certain actions lead towards suffering and certain actions lead towards happiness and eventually towards release. So we are conditioned, but we're able to change our conditioning through discernment, through wisdom. And the change is brought about by an act of will. So it's a sort of in-between place. We're not completely free, obviously, but we are free to change our habits because we've created them through wrong understanding, through acts of will.

The way I describe it is like going on a walk. If you've got a map with you, you get to a point where the road bifurcates and you don't quite know where to go. So you look at the map and see you've got to go right. It's the same with us. The road is, as it were, towards liberation. Once we're on that road, it's towards liberation. But there are all sorts of little bypasses and forks where we occasionally get lost. And it's through discernment, through wise reflection—yoniso manasikāra—and through our internal experiences that we find our way back. We come across things like stuff we've suppressed, little guilts that come up.

One that came up for me was quite remarkable actually. When I was about eighteen or nineteen, it was winter and I was coming home driving the old man's car—one of these great big Rovers, like a tank. As I came round into the street where we lived, the street before—so you come off the main road, turn right, then first turn left—the car very gently slid across. I got home, and the next morning my mother said there'd been a crash at the road and somebody had knocked the wall over of this neighbour. I went out and took the brickwork off the mudguard. And I said nothing.

Then, when I'm in my middle forties, I'm sitting there meditating and suddenly this huge sort of horror arises in me—of not having taken responsibility for it, of what might have happened, all of that. I'd obviously buried that sense of shock in the system. So it's not as though you get away with anything eventually.

That's definitely not the Buddha's teaching anyway. He's not into fiddling. He actually talks about shame and guilt as being the guardians of society—the guardians, not the guards—they're not wicked, they're there to tell us. As we become more and more refined and do something which we feel is hurtful, to the other or to ourselves or to both, we immediately get that sense of having done something wrong—guilt. This isn't the great existential guilt that I shouldn't be here anyway because I'm so evil. It's just: I did something wrong. And shame—you feel a bit ashamed about it. So you have to put that right to clear the heart. In that way, shame and guilt become the guardians of society.

From a Buddhist point of view, then, scientism leads to a sort of dead end. And one thing that occurs to me—you don't know, of course—but it occurs to me that if I were truly a materialist, thinking that at some point I'll die and that'll be the end of it, I can't imagine myself having put so much energy into the practice, especially when it got incredibly difficult. I would have thought, well, this is ridiculous. I might as well go out and have a pint. Do something useful.

I think that's a big block. And of course these things are self-reinforcing, aren't they? Eventually you have to justify the fact that you're not putting a great deal of effort into the practice because, well, life's short and I'm not going to get fully liberated anyway, so what's the point? I may as well be a little liberated and make my way as best I can. In a sense it undermines the great project for us. It's underselling the Buddha's teachings.

So that's the first avenue through which Buddhism is being received—through science. And of course we now have this mindfulness movement, which has brought it into medicine and mental health. Occasionally somebody actually wants to find out where this comes from. I do get people very occasionally who come from having done mindfulness and stress reduction or something and they've taken it a bit further. And although sometimes people criticise it because it's not taught with morality and such, the fact is it does people good, so don't knock it. That's my own personal opinion, of course.
So now we have Romanticism. Thanissaro Bhikkhu, whom some of you might know, has written a very good book on it. Here I just want to pull out one or two things to show us.

The Romantic movement was a reaction to the scientific revolution and what we call the Enlightenment—the rise of rationalism. You have Newton telling us that everything is just mechanical and God is deus ex machina, the God outside the machine who sort of set it going and let it run. You've got Kant telling us we shouldn't really trust our feelings, that we should be rational, that morality is a rational thing. You've got Descartes telling us that animals don't have any feelings, don't feel anything. And then there's a reaction against all that—a movement back to the heart.
The great philosophers of the Romantic movement were really the Germans—Novalis and Schleiermacher. I'm just reducing everything here to the basics of what they taught.

The first thing, of course, was back to nature. The Industrial Revolution had taken people out from nature and stuck them in these dreadful factories, satanic mills and all that. There was a loss of connection with nature, and therefore a sense of alienation—alienation brought about, as Marx described it, by doing things that weren't connected to you, making things you couldn't even buy. Life had become very rigid; it wasn't with the seasons anymore.

I remember a woman who came to the Vihara in Birmingham saying that there was a point where they brought electricity to the farm and they lost time. That's how she expressed what happened to her as a young girl—or she might have been a bit older. In the old days, say during winter, you'd have this vast amount of darkness and time where you could tell stories and such. Then suddenly you've got light and you're back up at five o'clock milking cows. She expressed it as a loss of time.

So these Romantics tried to get people back to nature, and the first thing was the love of it. I'm just quoting Wordsworth here, that very famous poem: 'I wandered lonely as a cloud that floats on high o'er vales and hills, when all at once I saw a crowd, a host of golden daffodils, beside the lake, beneath the trees, fluttering and dancing.'

You see a movement back to nature, and with it the feeling that we begin to understand nature as an organic whole—this is way before James Lovelock and his Gaia theory. We are part of that organic whole. The interesting thing is that although there's purpose in the universe, there's no specific purpose for human beings. The Romantics also accepted a lot of science; they didn't reject it. So I think a lot of them were atheists, were what we would call annihilationists.

All they knew was that if you gave yourself to nature, if you went back to it and meditated, and that sense of awe and wonder came, then you would have this experience of oneness—this oceanic feeling. Freud dismissed that as something coming up from being a baby, but for the Romantics, getting lost in the oceanic feeling was the height of spiritual endeavour. Through practice you could make it last a bit longer, but it definitely didn't last forever. And eventually you died.

It was the idea that the individual was mirrored in nature and nature was mirrored in the individual. The purpose of life was to find that connection and be reintegrated, to lose that sense of separateness. And that's what happened when you went into this oceanic feeling of being at one with nature.

I have to say I had a very strange experience, for me. I was up in the hills of Sri Lanka. I came out of a long retreat—I think I was up there for a couple of months—and I climbed this hill. The beauty of the landscape was such that I couldn't stop crying. I was crying my eyes out. It was almost too painful to be able to see.

This reminds me of a video you can see of Ajaan Maha Boowa, who has told us he doesn't suffer, he's an Arahant. While he's telling people about that moment where he was completely liberated, he remembers the joy, and he can't stop crying. This old man, he must be in his nineties, crying, telling people how wonderful it was. And of course all these critics say no Arahant can cry, it's ridiculous. He lambasts them. He's written an essay saying, how do you know how to have a response to life?

That was it for the Romantics. The deepest suffering was this feeling of alienation from nature and the evolutionary process—they believed in Darwinism. But it could be alleviated momentarily, and we can all achieve this. We can all find our way. There's no set way to do it, no particular religion. You just had to find your way to get lost into this feeling.

The other thing, of course, was that the ethics tended to follow from being part of that organic whole. There was no free will; the idea that you were making choices was a delusion. So again we go back to this idea of being completely conditioned, without any sense of free will.

And this whole idea that life is about experience—that's what we get from the Romantics. It comes right through to our liberal society: what we're working for is experience. The holiday. Visiting Machu Picchu and the Norwegian fjords. And if you haven't seen the Northern Lights up in the Arctic, then frankly, you haven't lived. You've got to display your T-shirts. Life is about these peak experiences.

Consider this: you're a young boy, a young girl, brought up in a village where the routine is very regular, going through the various seasons—the cold season, the hot season, the rainy season. Then around the age of eight you decide you'd like to join a monastery, and you join the monastery. You don't play any games. You have times in the day where you do a bit of study, you chant together, you eat together, you sleep together, and you get up too early and feel tired.

And you just go through year after year. About the age of twenty-one you become a monk and carry on doing the same things. Sometimes you give a talk, sometimes you don't. The bell goes at three o'clock in the morning and you get up and do a bit of meditation or wander about a bit. Then you turn up for breakfast and it's the same old breakfast. It never changes. Sometimes you get a delightful something that turns up. But generally speaking, you wander out and it's similar conversations about the weather and all that.

Then you wander back to your hut and do a bit of meditation. The bell goes for lunch and you get your bowl ready. You walk up, get your lunch, and go back again. You might have a kip in the middle of the day, you might read a bit, you might meditate a bit. You might meet up with people and go for evening chanting. There's a cup of tea or something. Then in the evening you do a bit more meditation, a bit more chanting. Then you go to sleep and get ready to get up again next morning at three o'clock. Every day, on and on. And then suddenly you're very old and you get a cough and you drop dead.

Is that a worthwhile life from a Romantic point of view? They've hardly walked up a mountain. They've never played a game. They've never got drunk. They've never had sex. Oh my God. Can you imagine that? Going through life without having had sex.

When you contrast that with the way we expect to live in a liberal society, it's just completely different. A different aim altogether. And yet this little boy may become fully liberated and live in eternal bliss, while we will die with tremendous guilt and horror. It's something to contemplate: how much of our lives are driven by trying to get what Maslow calls peak experience?

And that's what we come to next: psychotherapy. Psychotherapy turns everything into some sort of medical problem. It's not really a moral problem. Everything becomes a medical or social problem. The idea of morality doesn't really figure very highly in most psychotherapies.

We go back to Freud, who of course had the idea that you had this subconscious, this id, that you didn't know about, and it was constantly coming into consciousness—your ego. And this figure above you, which was society, what you'd learned, trying to guide you. Your job was to try and find a way through that morass coming at you all the time: uncontrollable urges and the voice of God coming down upon you.
Then you've got people like Carl Jung. Alfred Adler thought it was all about power. Carl Jung went a little bit deeper, I think, into all this universal consciousness and such.

Then you get a break from that with behaviourism, where people like Skinner only looked at your behaviour. The whole idea of an internal life just went by the board. It was all about conditioning—trying to condition people. And Skinner had some very horrible ideas about bringing up children and making them do things, conditioning them, definitely not giving them love. That would make them soft.

And then there was the great Dr. Spock in the sixties—the damn doctor, I think he was—who brought out this book which has sold millions. I mean fifty million; it's still in print. He actually says parents should trust themselves and they should love their baby. Not try and train it. If you read about it, it's all very strange. Huge swings within the whole development of psychotherapy.

But the one I want to point out is Maslow, because Maslow is a humanist psychotherapist. He looked at people who were having a good time, instead of people who had issues. And the thing I want to point out to you is this peak experience.

When somebody has a peak experience, all these things seemingly happen at the same time. There's a loss of judgment about time and space—you lose your sense of where and when you are. There's a feeling of being one whole and harmonious self, free of dissociation or inner conflict. A feeling of using all your capacities and capabilities at their highest potential, of being fully functioning—functioning effortlessly and easily without strain or struggle. Feeling completely responsible for your perceptions and behaviour. The use of self-determination to become stronger, more single-minded, fully volitional. Being without inhibition, fear, doubt, or self-criticism. Spontaneous expressiveness, naturally flowing behaviour that isn't constrained by conformity. A free mind that's flexible and open to creative thoughts and ideas. Complete mindfulness of the present moment without influence of past or expected future experiences. And a physical feeling of warmth along with pleasant vibrations emanating from the heart area outwards into the limbs.

Wow. Sounds very much like Arahantship, I think. So at least there's a movement here pointing to something of a higher order. But again, it's all to do with the mind, all to do with feeling, all to do with the way you think and the way you function. There's no hint of anything that might be transcendent of that.

It's not as though psychotherapy is against what we're doing with vipassana. It's just that there's a different aim. Psychotherapy, generally speaking, as I understand it, is about finding an accommodation to life—working with difficult situations like marriage counselling and such, trying to work through mental states that we're suffering from: panic, depression, all that sort of stuff. And in that, of course, it's very successful and very helpful.

In the East, you see, if you get very, very depressed or you have things like panic attacks, you might go to a monastery and they'll just tell you to practise metta. There's not much in Buddhist teaching that I've come across which is to do with helping people with severe mental problems. It would all be put down to karma—the idea that this life you're bearing the karma of what you did wrong in the last life, so don't worry, when you die you'll be born somewhere else and you'll be much happier. There's this notion that you're suffering something from some past action.

So it's really looking at the world from a different angle if we're going to look at it spiritually. Instead of looking bottom up through the physical nature and then up and up and up, thinking that everything arises out of that, if you look down—coming down from, say, this transcendent experience—then you get a different thing altogether. And again, lots of stuff is inexplicable. For instance, the process of rebirth, how you move from one place to another upon death, is not described in the scriptures. It's talked about, it's spoken of, it's actually said that this is what happens, but the actual process isn't described. So there are lots of areas left, probably, to our imaginations.

So again, Ananda says: 'I have heard that on one occasion the Blessed One was staying near Savatthi'—same place—'at Jeta's Grove.' It was his favourite monastery, Anathapindika's monastery. 'And on that occasion, the Blessed One was instructing, urging, rousing, and encouraging monks with Dhamma talk concerned with liberation. And the monks, receptive, attentive, focusing their attention, lending an ear, listened to the Dhamma.' And on realising the significance of this, the Buddha on that occasion exclaimed...

So there is a dimension, there is a sphere. Now remember this word ayatana—you chant it every morning in dependent origination: salayatana, the six sense spheres. The thing about spheres is that you can't mix them; you can't hear through your eyes, for instance. They are distinct experiences, these ayatana. The word here, 'dimension,' is rather nice.

What he's saying is that there is this dimension. This isn't something, shall we say, poetic—this isn't a metaphor. He's actually using the same word that he uses for what we experience through the senses, the six senses, the sixth being the mind.

So there is—and again, I should have mentioned this with the earlier quote I gave—there are two words in Pali for 'is.' One is what you call a copulative. It's like when you say 'this is a table' or 'this is an electric fire.' It's just something to join two words, and the word for that is hoti. But when you say 'is' to mean 'exists,' then the word is atti. So when they ask the Buddha, 'Does the Tathagata exist after he dies, or does he not exist?'—the word is atti. And this is the word he uses here. He says there is a dimension.

Now he says: 'where there's neither earth, water, nor fire, nor wind.' So again, nothing of the physical world. But notice he doesn't go beyond what we can sense of the physical world. Remember, these are referring to qualities—to pressure, fluidity, some sort of temperature, and movement.

Then he says there's neither the dimension—neither the same sphere, because that's also called a sphere—of these higher jhanas: the four arupa jhanas, the infinitude of space, infinitude of consciousness, the dimension of nothingness, and the place of perception or non-perception. So even the highest level that you can take the mind to, in its most refined state, is not there. That's not what he's talking about.

And he says it's neither of this world nor the next world. Now he's being more physical, in a sense, by saying there's no sun nor moon. So it's something completely transcendent—other than, right? Other than.

And there, he says, there's neither coming nor going nor staying—no time, no space. There's no passing away nor arising, so nothing transient. In fact, there's nothing there from this side of experience at all. He says it's not fixed, not moving, not evolving. Nothing's happening there. And it's without support—in other words, it's not conditioned, not dependent on anything else. Just this, he says, is the end of suffering.

That's a really clear statement of something utterly transcendent of what we normally experience. And of course, that's what we are backing into. Every time you're practising meditation and observing something, you're turning it into an object, which means the subject—this sense of the observer—is coming back. It's moving backwards towards a cliff. And the cliff happens to be a great place.

Within Buddhism we have what's called the Tathāgatagarbha theory, from the Yogācāra school. The idea here is that the Tathāgata, the Buddha within, finds itself within a psychophysical organism—not necessarily a human one. And remember, it doesn't have any mass. Nibbāna doesn't have any mass; there's no earth. So it's as comfortable in a little microbe as it is in an elephant. Or a dinosaur. It just fills it. It's just there with it.

So this finds itself in there and makes the mistake, of course, of believing itself to be this. And then comes the urge to seek happiness, the urge to seek liberation—either impelled from within by some intuitive sense that there must be something beyond, or simply from living in the world where suffering arises. We're always trying to escape suffering. And when you realise there are pleasures about, and you're happy when there are pleasures about, naturally you seek more pleasure. For me it's a very straightforward psychological dynamic. I don't think there's anything too stressful about understanding that.

But then, because of that process, this tathāgata, this garbha, this embryo, begins to grow and eventually ends up as the Tathāgata. The word means—well, the basic terms translate as either 'thus come' or 'thus gone.' But the best translation points to something beyond. He uses other imagery too, like the island. So when he talks about himself as the Tathāgata, he's talking about something that has transcended.

How this came about, how the phenomenal universe came about, is never discussed. All the Buddha points to is that we're here and we're in a mess, and he knows how to get us out of it. It's a very restricted teaching in that sense. He doesn't go beyond that. He doesn't really talk about physics or biology or anything like that. It's not in his purview. It's not what he's actually on about.

He sees clearly through the Four Noble Truths when he states it: this is an unsatisfactory situation. The problem is how do we get out of that? Is there an escape? And that's what he discovers. His whole teaching is wedged towards this escape.

Lots of things are not explained. Lots of things are not scientific in the way we would like them to be. The whole idea of rebirth, the whole idea of Nibbāna—these are unscientific. You're never going to prove them.

Eventually it does boil down to a certain confidence that he wasn't having us on, and then to putting effort into the practice and beginning to see—even at the level of the role of desire—that there is an escape at the point of desire. You don't have to follow it. You can actually move your whole conditioning towards something more beneficial.

That's about it, I think. We've got these three ways that we look at something coming towards us, something new from outside our culture. We try to make it fit into our science. We try to make it fit into this background idea, a romantic view of what life's actually about. And it has to fit in some way into our psychotherapy.

Once we're clear as to where all these three things are, and where the Buddhist teaching is actually isolated within that—once we gain that clarity—then of course our progress is going to be much better. We won't be sidelined. We'll feel that we don't need to come to conclusions. If we don't know, why come to a conclusion?

I was on a train recently, last year, going down to Brighton, and a rugby crowd got on. These two guys sat opposite me, and one of them said, 'You're a Buddhist monk, aren't you?' I said, 'I am.' He said, 'Well, I don't believe in rebirth or reincarnation.' I said, 'Do you know whether there's reincarnation or not?' He said, 'No.' I said, 'Well, why make a conclusion?' And his mate said, 'Yeah, why make a conclusion?' He'd just decided there's no such thing as reincarnation. We had a great old conversation. He still didn't believe in it, of course, but at least I might have halted his... Well, it's pride, isn't it? It's all hubris: 'I know. It doesn't make sense to me, so it can't be.'
As long as we remain in that humble place—truthful to ourselves, willing to say 'I don't know'—then we leave open the possibility of exploration, the possibility of insight.
